Fnrn PolrrrcAl Pnncrrcps CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento. CA 9-581+-2329
(916) 322--5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
September 28,2OlO
Joe Kalmick
1603 Seal Way
Seal Beach,CA 90740
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-154
Dear Mr. Kalmick:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when
it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Since you have not provided facts
regarding the specific decision, we can only offer informal assistance.2
Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not
address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest or Govemment Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
lf elected to the Seal Beach City Council, would you be able to participate in decisions
regarding the main street specific plan?
CONCLUSION
Absent facts regarding a specific decision, we can only advise that if a decision
materially affects your property or business, you will be required to disqualify yourself. This
determination in made on a case-by-case basis according to the facts pertaining to the particular
I The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statubry
ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
I Intbrmal assistance does not provide the requeskrr with the immunity provided by an opini<-rn or tbrmal
written advice. (Section 83 I 14, Regulation I 8329(c)(3).)


File No. I-10-154
Page No. 2
matter. Without the factual context of a specific decision, we are unable to provide advice that is
more specific.
FACTS
You are a candidate for the Seal Beach City Council. You are a resident of the OId Town
district and you own a business on Main Street. The Main Street Business District is
approximately three blocks and starts at the Pacific Coast Highway and ends at the Seal Beach
Pier.
Questions have been raised as to whether your business ownership would preclude you
from voting on issues relating to Main Street and the Main Street Specific plan. In our telephone
conversation of September 23,2010, you provided additional facts:
o Your business is a picture framing business.
o You currently lease the property on from which the business is operated and intend to
change locations (to one still on Main Street) and will acquire the new property under a
long-term lease.
o The types of decisions that you anticipate being confronted with as a city councilmember
include decisions affecting landscaping or lighiing and decisions setting or extending
hours of operation for businesses or restaurants on Main street.
o You also note that Main Street is currently built out and saturated. Currently there is a
moratorium on certain new businesses.
ANALYSIS
The Act seeks to avoid conflicts of interest in govemmental decisions. Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits a public official at any level of state or local govem.n.ri fro- -uting,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his official pisition to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he iras a financial interest.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700.) An official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that theiecision will have
a material financial effect on one or more of the official's economic interests. (Section g7lO3;
Regulation 18700(a).)
To determine if an individual has a disqualifying financial interest under the Act, the
Commission applies the following eighrstep analysis. ir tn" criteria at any of the steps is not
met, a conflict of interest will not exist. For example if an official has no economic interest in
the decision (step 3) the analysis ends and no .orflict of interest exist. Similarly, if the official
does have an economic interest in the decision but the effect of the decision on it " 
economic
interest will not be material, then the analysis ends and the official will not have a conflict of
interest.


File No. I-10-154
Page No. 3
Step One: Is the individual a "public official,,?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "pnblic officials." (Section
87100.) Defined in Section 82048(a), "public official" means every member, officer, employee,
or consultant of a state or local government agency. If elected to the Seal Beach City Council,
you will be considered a public official.
Step Two: Is the public official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
In determining whether a conflict of interest may be present, a govemmental decision
needs to be identified, as well as the public official's actions in relation to that decision.
o A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints u p"rron, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency, or determines not to act. (Regulation lg7o2.l.)
o { public official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position, the official negotiates, without significant substantive review,
with a governmental entity or private person regarding a governmental decision, or advises or
makes recommendations to the decision maker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, including conducting research or an investigation, or
preparing or presenting a report, analysis or opinion. (Regulation 18702.2(i)(l) and (2).)
A public official is "attempting to influence a governmental decision" through his or her
official position if the official contacts or appears before, or otherwise attem-pts to influence
any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the agency of which he or she is employed.
(Regulation 18702.3.) Further, a public official is attempting to influence the decision of an
agency of which he or she is not employed when pu.portirg to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency in the same manner as described above. (Regulation
r8702.3(b).)
a
You specifically ask whether you may make or participate in specific decisions of the city
council. Therefore, this element would be satisfied.r
i If a public oft'icial's otfice is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 filers" include members of a city council)
and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( l) immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic intirest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest. as discussed in Regulation 1g702.5(b)( l)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2)
recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and./or vote on the item. For
closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests,
special rules tbund in Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Seciion g7ld5.)


File No. I-10-154
Page No.4
Step Three: Does the public official have one of the five types of economic interests?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions identify five general categories of economic
interests as enumerated in Regulations 18703.1 through 1870a.5. These flve general categories
are: business entities, real property, sources of incomL, sources of gifts, and piersonal finances.
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or if the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position in management. (Section 87103(a) and (d); tiegulation
18703.1(a) and (b).)
2. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Secrion g7l03G); Regulation 1g703.2.) Section
82033 defines an "interest in real property" to include any "leasehoii, beneficial or ownership
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real prop.rty located in the jurisdiction owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public officiai, oi othu filer, or his oi her immediate
family if the fair market value of the inrerest is two thousand dollars ($ 2,ooo; ". *;r; . . ..;.
3. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) If you have received any income of $500 or more from
your business, and this income was received within twelve months of any governmental decision
you were involved with under Step Two, the business as well as clients inJividually supplying
$500 or more of income would qualify as economic interests.
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $420 or more within twelve months prior to the decision. (Section g7103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
5. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and those of
his or her immediate family. A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic
interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, assets, income, or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family increasing oi decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation
18703.5.)
Your question concems:
o Business interests (investments in, employment with, and income received from) . please
note that "income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a
spouse and a pro rata share of the income of any businesi "niity o, trust in which the official
(or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a l0-percent or greater
interest. (Section 82030(a). )
{ Regulation 18233 states that "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest" as used in Government
Code Section 82033 do not include the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month gr less.


File No. I-10-154
Page No. 5
o Real property you lease.s we assume fo.r purposes of this letter that your leasehold interest
in the property is worth $2,000 or more.6 ^ '
o d public official always lras an interest in his or her personal finances as well as those of his
or her immediate family.T (Section 87103.) Reasonably foreseeable financial effects on a
public official's or his or her immediate.family's p"rronal finances are deemed material if the
effect is at least $250 in any twelve monrh p"iioa. (Regularion 1g705.5(a).) However,
generally, when determining whether a governmental dicision has a material financial effect
on a public official's personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real
property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross
revenues' expenses' or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official
has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered. Therefore, we do not further
analyze this economic interest.
step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision?
To determine whether the effect of a governmental decision is reasonably foreseeable and
material, you first must determine whether th-e interest is directly or indirectlylnvolved in the
decision. (Regulation 1870a(a).)
Business Enttties and Sources of Income.: Economic interests in ..business entities,, and
"sources of income" ate directly involved in a govemrn.niut decision before the official,s agency
as follows: 
v'r vvrvrv Lrr!
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in i decision before an official,, og"r"y'*t Ln that
person, either directly or by an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is.the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's og.n"y.'e person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuancJ, .".r"*ui, approvar, o"riir, *
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person." (Regulation lS7O4.t.)
s You also noted. in.your letter that you own a residence in the jurisdiction that is located more than 500 feet
from Main Street, which is the fbcus of youiinquiry. t;;;;;; ; ori.a ouort and we have not anaryzedconfli*s
:Jrl[T::: 
that might arise in connection wittr city iouncil decisions uir."ting property ,iii,ut"i'n.o, your personat
6 The value of the leasehold is the amount of rent owed during a l2-month period. (Regulation ls72g.)
7 Section 82029 defines "immediate family" to include the pubric official's spouse and dependent children.


File No. I-10-154
Page No.6
This standard would apply whether it was your business that was the applicant or a client
who has paid you or your business $500 or more in the l2months prior to the decision.
The economic interest will be deemed inclirectly involved in the governmental decision if
found not to be directly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).) For purpos"r-of thi, letter, we assume
your questions do not involve decisions in which you business or clients are directly involved.
Real Property: Undet Section 1870a.1(a)(1), real properry is directly involved in a
govemmental decision if:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the prlposed
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision. For
purposes of subdivision (a)(5), real property is located ;within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of thereal property which is the subject of
the govemmental decision' if any part of the reai pr-opr.ty is within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaiies) of the redevelopment project area.
"(2) The govemmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or-exclusion
from any city, county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real
property in which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the
real property. For purposes of this subdivision, the terms ,.zoning', and
"rezoning" shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the z6ning or land use
designation on the real property in which the officiat his an interest.
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or Llses
of the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real pioperty in
which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, tolorm a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the redevelop*"nt plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it
is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment area.


File No. t-10-154
Page No.7
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer, storrn drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which
the official has an interest will receive new or improved services.i'
Depending on the facts of a specific decision, your real property interest could be directly
involved in the decision. You noted that your property is on Main Street, thus your property
would be within 500 feet of properties subject to some Main Street decisions. Furthir, if yort
property is in the specific plan area, your property would be directly involved in some decisions
affecting the specific plan.
The regulation does provide a series of limited exceptions:
"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision,
bfi is instead indirectly involved if:
"(1) The decision solely concems the amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as changes in the ur"r per-itted, or
development standards applicable, within a particular zoning category) which is
applicable to all other properties designated in that category, which shall be
analyzed under 2 Cal. Code Regs. section 18705.2(b).
"(2) The decision solely concerns repairs, replacement, or maintenance of
existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities.
"(3) The decision solely concerns the adoption or amendment of a general
plan and all of the following apply:
"(A) The decision only identifies planning objectives or is otherwise
exclusively one of policy. A decision will not qualify under this subdivision if the
decision is initiated by the public official, by a person that is an economic interest
of the public official, or by a person representing either the public official or an
economic interest of the public official.
"(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public
official's agency prior to implementing the planning or policy objettives-.
Examples of further decisions include, but are not limited to, permitting,
licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or change to a zoning variance, Iand use
ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent.
"(C) The decision does not concern an identifiable parcel or parcels or
development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel or
parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which the decision
is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area depicted on a map or
diagram offered in connection with the decision, provided that ihe map or


File No. t-10-154
Page No. 8
diagram depicts all parcels located within the agency's jurisdiction and economic
interests of the official are not singled out.
"(D) The decision does not concern the agency's prior, concurrent, or
subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, li-cense, zoning designation,
zoning variance, rand use ordinance, or specific pran or i,. .qiiroi"ii.,,
IEmphasis added.]
lf the exception should apply, your interest in real property would be analyzed as
indirectly involved in the decision. 
-
Again' without specific facts related to a specific decision to consider, it is not possible to
determine if these exceptions may apply.
Steps Five and six: wi[ the governmental decision have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on the public official,s ".or,o-i. interest?
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a)') An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 1g706(a).) whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made
depends on the facts-surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Business Entities and sources of Income: lf abusiness entity is directly involved in a
decision as an applic*J-o-t the subject of a decision, the financial effect is presumed to be
material' (Regulation 18705.l(bxl)') Thispresumptionmay be rebutred *itt, o showing that it
is not reasonably foreseeable that there.would be any rinanciat effect on the business entity .
(Regulation 18705'1(b)(l).) You stated in_our telepirone conversation of Septemb er 23,2010,
that you understood this strict standard and were not o*ting about these types of decisions.
In all other cases, the economic interest is indirectly involved in the decision. where the
interest is indirectly involved, Regulation l8T05.l(c) prouid", different stanaaros to apply to
determine if a financial effect is material.based o" irr"'ryp" and size of business in question. For
example, for a small business entity that is not publicatiy t.ooeo and has less than $750,000 in
net income during the most recent fiscal year,Iiegulation 18705.1(cXa) applies and provides that
a financial effect is considered material if:
."(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,


File No. I-10-154
Page No.9
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducinfor rii.i"oring existinjffinses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, *orr-; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or riuuiii,i"s of $20,000 or more.,,
The types of decisions you described **-lq not appear to- affect your business materially.
However, again this woulddepend on the facts of the puii'"ur* decision.
Real Propefi: lor directly involved real property, Regulation 1g705.2(aXl) provides
that rhe financial effect of a govemmentar decisiili";;;;d to be material.
"The.pres.umption may be rgbyted byproof that it is not reasonabry
foreseeable thl the gor"t mirrtal decision *iir t ur" any financial effect on the
real property." (Regulation I g70S.Zf"itiU
under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a one-penny
effect. This is hoy, as the."one-penny ruIe.,, h ";J;;;;;;";;ffi;br, 
the official
must establish that the decision would not affect his or her property interest by even one cent.

involved in a decision 6ut is.only liased by the "rnri.r. Thr;rd;6ti"" 
"i*rterialiry can be

o change the legally allowable use of the Ieased real property, and the lessee
has a right to sublease the real property;
o Change the lessee's actual use of the real property;

of the leased real property;
o Increase or decrease the amount of rent for the leased real property by 5
.percent during any l2-month period foilowing the decision; or t - )

of course, if an exception applies or the site of the decision is more than 500 feet away
from your property, you would rpptt the indirectly involved analysis for materiality. The
financial effect of a govemmental dicision on r"uipirp*y *rru, is indirectly involved in the
governmental decision is presumed not to be material.'This presumption may be rebutted by

effect, and the nature_of th9 leal-Rroperty thalmake"it d;;i;ffi;ffi]5", 
*" decision
will have a material financial effictbn tie real proprrty ir*rhicl the prbii";ifi;ial has an


File No. I-10-154
Page No. 10
interest. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).) In rebutting the presumption that the effect would not be
material, you would consider the same factors set fort[r above.
Again, without a specific decision, we cannot advise with any certainty.
Steps Seven and Eight: The Public Generally Exception and Legally Required
Participation
. 
Even if Steps one through Six suggest a disqualifying conflict of interest, the economic
interest is not disqualifying if the official &n establiih ttrat the govemmental decision will affect
their interest in a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will
affect the public generally. (Regulation 18707(a).) This analysis is set forth in Regulations
18707 'l through 18707.9. However, due to the relatively small size of the Main Street Specific
Plan, it appears unlikely that the exception would apply io those types of decisions if a conflict of
interest were to exist.
Similarly, if the official can establish that they are legally required to make or participate
in making the governmental decision in question, noiwithstanding the potential disqualifying
interest' the official may take part in such decision, subject to reporting requirements.
(Regulation 18708(a).) We cannot address this question unless you uri elected and an actual
conflict of interest arises.
Segmentation. Finally, we have previously advised that large and complex decisions
may, under certain circumstances, be divided into slparate decisions so that an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still participate as to other
components in which the official has no financial interest. -1MeriuloffAdri." 
Letter, No. I-90-
542') This process is called "segmentation." Regulation ttizog prouia", the procedures for
segmentation and states as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, prouid"d all of the
following conditions apply:
"(l) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; and


File No. I-10-154
Page No. 1l
"(4) once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has
been made, the disqualified public official's puni.ipotion does not result in a
reopening of, or otherwise financially affect,ihe decision from which the official
was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are .inextricably
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm,
nullify, or alter the result of another decision.,,
Segmentation is available only if a decision can be broken down into separate decisions that are
not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which the officiar has a disqualifying conflict of
interest' The commission has advised that if the resolution or one decislon ;iil in any wcty affect
the decision from which a public official is disqualified it, a""irions may not be segmented.
(Barker Advice Letter, No. 4-03-022; Mclaughtrn raui""-L.n"r, No. a_bz_t 32.) Again,
application of this rule is necessarily fact dependent.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, prease contact me
at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By: John W. Wallace
General Counsel
Division

sections.question:
lf elected to the Seal Beach City Council, would you be able to participate in decisions
regarding the main street specific plan?

materially affects your property or business, you will be required to disqualify yourself. This
determination in made on a case-by-case basis according to the facts pertaining to the particular

matter. Without the factual context of a specific decision, we are unable to provide advice that is
more specific.

You are a candidate for the Seal Beach City Council. You are a resident of the OId Town
district and you own a business on Main Street. The Main Street Business District is
approximately three blocks and starts at the Pacific Coast Highway and ends at the Seal Beach
Pier.
Questions have been raised as to whether your business ownership would preclude you
from voting on issues relating to Main Street and the Main Street Specific plan. In our telephone
conversation of September 23,2010, you provided additional facts:
o Your business is a picture framing business.
o You currently lease the property on from which the business is operated and intend to
change locations (to one still on Main Street) and will acquire the new property under a
long-term lease.
o The types of decisions that you anticipate being confronted with as a city councilmember
include decisions affecting landscaping or lighiing and decisions setting or extending
hours of operation for businesses or restaurants on Main street.
o You also note that Main Street is currently built out and saturated. Currently there is a
moratorium on certain new businesses.
sections.analysis:
The Act seeks to avoid conflicts of interest in govemmental decisions. Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits a public official at any level of state or local govem.n.ri fro- -uting,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his official pisition to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he iras a financial interest.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700.) An official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that theiecision will have
a material financial effect on one or more of the official's economic interests. (Section g7lO3;
Regulation 18700(a).)
To determine if an individual has a disqualifying financial interest under the Act, the
Commission applies the following eighrstep analysis. ir tn" criteria at any of the steps is not
met, a conflict of interest will not exist. For example if an official has no economic interest in
the decision (step 3) the analysis ends and no .orflict of interest exist. Similarly, if the official
does have an economic interest in the decision but the effect of the decision on it " 
economic
interest will not be material, then the analysis ends and the official will not have a conflict of
interest.
Step One: Is the individual a "public official,,?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "pnblic officials." (Section
87100.) Defined in Section 82048(a), "public official" means every member, officer, employee,
or consultant of a state or local government agency. If elected to the Seal Beach City Council,
you will be considered a public official.
Step Two: Is the public official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
In determining whether a conflict of interest may be present, a govemmental decision
needs to be identified, as well as the public official's actions in relation to that decision.
o A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints u p"rron, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency, or determines not to act. (Regulation lg7o2.l.)
o { public official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position, the official negotiates, without significant substantive review,
with a governmental entity or private person regarding a governmental decision, or advises or
makes recommendations to the decision maker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, including conducting research or an investigation, or
preparing or presenting a report, analysis or opinion. (Regulation 18702.2(i)(l) and (2).)
A public official is "attempting to influence a governmental decision" through his or her
official position if the official contacts or appears before, or otherwise attem-pts to influence
any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the agency of which he or she is employed.
(Regulation 18702.3.) Further, a public official is attempting to influence the decision of an
agency of which he or she is not employed when pu.portirg to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency in the same manner as described above. (Regulation
r8702.3(b).)
a
You specifically ask whether you may make or participate in specific decisions of the city
council. Therefore, this element would be satisfied.r
i If a public oft'icial's otfice is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 filers" include members of a city council)
and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( l) immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic intirest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest. as discussed in Regulation 1g702.5(b)( l)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2)
recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and./or vote on the item. For
closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests,
special rules tbund in Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Seciion g7ld5.)
Step Three: Does the public official have one of the five types of economic interests?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions identify five general categories of economic
interests as enumerated in Regulations 18703.1 through 1870a.5. These flve general categories
are: business entities, real property, sources of incomL, sources of gifts, and piersonal finances.
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or if the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position in management. (Section 87103(a) and (d); tiegulation
18703.1(a) and (b).)
2. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Secrion g7l03G); Regulation 1g703.2.) Section
82033 defines an "interest in real property" to include any "leasehoii, beneficial or ownership
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real prop.rty located in the jurisdiction owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public officiai, oi othu filer, or his oi her immediate
family if the fair market value of the inrerest is two thousand dollars ($ 2,ooo; ". *;r; . . ..;.
3. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) If you have received any income of $500 or more from
your business, and this income was received within twelve months of any governmental decision
you were involved with under Step Two, the business as well as clients inJividually supplying
$500 or more of income would qualify as economic interests.
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $420 or more within twelve months prior to the decision. (Section g7103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
5. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and those of
his or her immediate family. A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic
interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, assets, income, or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family increasing oi decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation
18703.5.)

(or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a l0-percent or greater

Code Section 82033 do not include the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of one month gr less.

generally, when determining whether a governmental dicision has a material financial effect

property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross
revenues' expenses' or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official
has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered. Therefore, we do not further
analyze this economic interest.
step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the governmental

To determine whether the effect of a governmental decision is reasonably foreseeable and
material, you first must determine whether th-e interest is directly or indirectlylnvolved in the
decision. (Regulation 1870a(a).)

This standard would apply whether it was your business that was the applicant or a client
who has paid you or your business $500 or more in the l2months prior to the decision.
The economic interest will be deemed inclirectly involved in the governmental decision if
found not to be directly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).) For purpos"r-of thi, letter, we assume
your questions do not involve decisions in which you business or clients are directly involved.
Real Property: Undet Section 1870a.1(a)(1), real properry is directly involved in a
govemmental decision if:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the prlposed
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision. For

the govemmental decision' if any part of the reai pr-opr.ty is within 500 feet of the

"(2) The govemmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or-exclusion
from any city, county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real

real property. For purposes of this subdivision, the terms ,.zoning', and

designation on the real property in which the officiat his an interest.
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or Llses
of the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real pioperty in

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select

redevelopment area.

"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer, storrn drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which
the official has an interest will receive new or improved services.i'
Depending on the facts of a specific decision, your real property interest could be directly
involved in the decision. You noted that your property is on Main Street, thus your property
would be within 500 feet of properties subject to some Main Street decisions. Furthir, if yort
property is in the specific plan area, your property would be directly involved in some decisions
affecting the specific plan.
The regulation does provide a series of limited exceptions:
"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision,
bfi is instead indirectly involved if:
"(1) The decision solely concems the amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as changes in the ur"r per-itted, or
development standards applicable, within a particular zoning category) which is
applicable to all other properties designated in that category, which shall be
analyzed under 2 Cal. Code Regs. section 18705.2(b).
"(2) The decision solely concerns repairs, replacement, or maintenance of
existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities.
"(3) The decision solely concerns the adoption or amendment of a general
plan and all of the following apply:
"(A) The decision only identifies planning objectives or is otherwise
exclusively one of policy. A decision will not qualify under this subdivision if the
decision is initiated by the public official, by a person that is an economic interest
of the public official, or by a person representing either the public official or an
economic interest of the public official.
"(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public
official's agency prior to implementing the planning or policy objettives-.
Examples of further decisions include, but are not limited to, permitting,
licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or change to a zoning variance, Iand use
ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent.
"(C) The decision does not concern an identifiable parcel or parcels or
development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel or
parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which the decision
is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area depicted on a map or
diagram offered in connection with the decision, provided that ihe map or

diagram depicts all parcels located within the agency's jurisdiction and economic

"(D) The decision does not concern the agency's prior, concurrent, or
subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, li-cense, zoning designation,
zoning variance, rand use ordinance, or specific pran or i,. .qiiroi"ii.,,
IEmphasis added.]
lf the exception should apply, your interest in real property would be analyzed as
indirectly involved in the decision. 
-
Again' without specific facts related to a specific decision to consider, it is not possible to
determine if these exceptions may apply.
Steps Five and six: wi[ the governmental decision have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on the public official,s ".or,o-i. interest?
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a)') An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 1g706(a).) whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made
depends on the facts-surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Business Entities and sources of Income: lf abusiness entity is directly involved in a
decision as an applic*J-o-t the subject of a decision, the financial effect is presumed to be
material' (Regulation 18705.l(bxl)') Thispresumptionmay be rebutred *itt, o showing that it
is not reasonably foreseeable that there.would be any rinanciat effect on the business entity .
(Regulation 18705'1(b)(l).) You stated in_our telepirone conversation of Septemb er 23,2010,
that you understood this strict standard and were not o*ting about these types of decisions.
In all other cases, the economic interest is indirectly involved in the decision. where the
interest is indirectly involved, Regulation l8T05.l(c) prouid", different stanaaros to apply to
determine if a financial effect is material.based o" irr"'ryp" and size of business in question. For
example, for a small business entity that is not publicatiy t.ooeo and has less than $750,000 in

."(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in

fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, *orr-; or,

the value of the business entity's assets or riuuiii,i"s of $20,000 or more.,,
The types of decisions you described **-lq not appear to- affect your business materially.
However, again this woulddepend on the facts of the puii'"ur* decision.

that rhe financial effect of a govemmentar decisiili";;;;d to be material.

real property." (Regulation I g70S.Zf"itiU
under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a one-penny
effect. This is hoy, as the."one-penny ruIe.,, h ";J;;;;;;";;ffi;br,

of course, if an exception applies or the site of the decision is more than 500 feet away
from your property, you would rpptt the indirectly involved analysis for materiality. The

Again, without a specific decision, we cannot advise with any certainty.
Steps Seven and Eight: The Public Generally Exception and Legally Required
Participation
. 
Even if Steps one through Six suggest a disqualifying conflict of interest, the economic
interest is not disqualifying if the official &n establiih ttrat the govemmental decision will affect
their interest in a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will
affect the public generally. (Regulation 18707(a).) This analysis is set forth in Regulations
18707 'l through 18707.9. However, due to the relatively small size of the Main Street Specific
Plan, it appears unlikely that the exception would apply io those types of decisions if a conflict of
interest were to exist.
Similarly, if the official can establish that they are legally required to make or participate
in making the governmental decision in question, noiwithstanding the potential disqualifying
interest' the official may take part in such decision, subject to reporting requirements.
(Regulation 18708(a).) We cannot address this question unless you uri elected and an actual
conflict of interest arises.
Segmentation. Finally, we have previously advised that large and complex decisions
may, under certain circumstances, be divided into slparate decisions so that an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still participate as to other
components in which the official has no financial interest. -1MeriuloffAdri." 
Letter, No. I-90-
542') This process is called "segmentation." Regulation ttizog prouia", the procedures for
segmentation and states as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, prouid"d all of the
following conditions apply:
"(l) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; andl
"(4) once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has
been made, the disqualified public official's puni.ipotion does not result in a
reopening of, or otherwise financially affect,ihe decision from which the official
was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are .inextricably
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm,
nullify, or alter the result of another decision.,,
Segmentation is available only if a decision can be broken down into separate decisions that are
not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which the officiar has a disqualifying conflict of
interest' The commission has advised that if the resolution or one decislon ;iil in any wcty affect
the decision from which a public official is disqualified it, a""irions may not be segmented.
(Barker Advice Letter, No. 4-03-022; Mclaughtrn raui""-L.n"r, No. a_bz_t 32.) Again,
application of this rule is necessarily fact dependent.

CA 9-581+-2329 (916) 322--5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886 September 28,2OlO Joe Kalmick 1603 Seal Way Seal Beach,CA 90740 Re Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-10-154 Dear Mr. Kalmick: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Since you have not provided facts regarding the specific decision, we can only offer informal assistance.2 Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090. QUESTION lf elected to the Seal Beach City Council, would you be able to participate in decisions regarding the main street specific plan? CONCLUSION Absent facts regarding a specific decision, we can only advise that if a decision materially affects your property or business, you will be required to disqualify yourself. FACTS You are a candidate for the Seal Beach City Council. You are a resident of the OId Town district and you own a business on Main Street. The Main Street Business District is approximately three blocks and starts at the Pacific Coast Highway and ends at the Seal Beach Pier. Questions have been raised as to whether your business ownership would preclude you from voting on issues relating to Main Street and the Main Street Specific plan. In our telephone conversation of September 23,2010, you provided additional facts: o Your business is a picture framing business. o You currently lease the property on from which the business is operated and intend to change locations (to one still on Main Street) and will acquire the new property under a long-term lease. o The types of decisions that you anticipate being confronted with as a city councilmember include decisions affecting landscaping or lighiing and decisions setting or extending hours of operation for businesses or restaurants on Main
